content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 11,
1993
Wesley W. Peltzer
Smith
& Peltzer
751 E. Rancheros Drive,
Suite
2
San Marcos,
CA
92069
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93—064
Dear Mr. Peltzer:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Morro Hills Community Services District Director Elliott
Lott,
concerning his duties under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
-!
This letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
Is Director Lott prohibited from participating in
decisions developing district policy on encroachment permits?
2.
Is Director Lott prohibited from participating in
decisions concerning encroachment permits?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
He is not prohibited from participating in decisions
establishing district policy if that policy affects his interests
in the same manner as it affects the public generally.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—93—064
Page Two
2.
Director Lott is not prohibited from making or
participating in the making of decisions concerning the approval
or disapproval of encroachment permits on property other than his
own, unless those decisions will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on his own economic interests.
FACTS
The Morro Hills Community Services District (“District”)
maintains approximately 6.2 miles of roads in the southwestern
area of Fallbrook, California.
A number of property owners within
Morro Hills have encroached upon unused portions of rights—of—way
held by the District.
Approximately 160 of the 190 residences in
Morro Hills encroached on District rights-of-way in some manner.
Elliott Lott was one of those property owners.
In March of 1991, the Board of Directors of the District
adopted a new policy prohibiting certain encroachments and
requiring permits for all encroachments.
Shortly after this new
change in policy, the District filed suit against Elliott Lott.
Negotiations between the District and Mr. Lott were unsuccessful
and that lawsuit is still pending.
In November of 1992, Elliott Lott was elected to the
District’s Board of Directors.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decisionwithin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable
2 / that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
2/
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
A—93—064
Page Three
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103
Accordingly, Director Lott may not make, participate in
making or attempt to use his position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on his economic interests specified in Section
87103 above.
The Commission has developed regulations which set forth the
guidelines for determining when a decision will have a material
financial effect on financial interests.
If the decision involves
the issuance, denial or revocation of alicense, permit or other
land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of such
property, the decision would have a material financial effect on
the official’s property and the official would be prohibited from
participating in such decisions.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (B).)
Applying this analysis, Director Lott is prohibited from
participating in decisions affecting the issuance, denial, or
revocation of easement permits on his property.
As for
participating in similar decisions affecting property other than


Advice File No.
A-93--064
Page Four
his own which may have an indirect impact on his property, this
would be permitted so long as there is no foreseeable material
financial effect on his property.
(Regulation 18702.3 would be
applied to make this determination.)
In the establishment of policy on encroachment in general,
which does not directly affect Director Lott’s property,
Regulation 18702.1 is not be the appropriate standard to determine
materiality.
Director Lott’s property would be indirectly affected by
decisions concerning general encroachment policy.
Accordingly,
where the real property is not the subject of the decision, the
effect of the decision would be material if:
(1)
The real property in which the official has an
interest,
or any part of it, is located within a 300
foot radius of the boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the decision, unless the decision will
have no financial effect upon the official’s real
property interest;
(2)
The decision involves construction of,
or
improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm drainage
or similar facilities,
and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or
substantially improved services; or
(3)
The real property in which the official has an
interest is located outside a radius of 300 feet and any
part of the real property is located within a radius of
2,500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000.
Regulation 18702.3 (a)
Accordingly, Director Lott would not be permitted to
participate in decisions concerning specific encroachment policy
if the decisions will have a material financial effect on his real
property interests as described above, unless his interests are
affected in the same manner as the public generally.
Public officials with interests that will be materially
affected by a decision may still participate in the decision if
the decision’s effect on their property is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
For the “public
generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect the
official’s interests in substantially the same manner as it would
affect a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)


Advice File No.
A-93--064
Page Five
You indicated that Morro Hills consists entirely of
residences.
Of the 190 residences in the jurisdiction,
160
presently have structural or landscaping encroachments on the
District’s rights-of-way.
Even if the remaining 30 residences
were not subject to the requirement to seek permits,
84 percent of
the residences are presently subject to the District’s
encroachment policies.
This represents a significant segment of
the public.
The public generally test has been met if the
encroachment by the 160 residences are similar in size and nature.
Director Lott is not prohibited from participating in developing
policy regarding encroachment permits for the District.
As to Director Lott’s ability to participate in decisions
concerning individual applications for encroachment permits,
he
would not be permitted to participate in such decision affecting
his own real property interests, nor would he be able to
participate if if the applicant was a source of income to him
under Section 87103(a)
above,
or if his real property interests
would be indirectly affected as described in Regulation 18702.3.
I trust this answer your questions.
If we can be of further
assistance, please feel free to contact the Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
al Counsel
Jeanette E.
11
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JM/JET/jt

financial effect on his own economic interests.

QUESTION: 1.
Is Director Lott prohibited from participating in
decisions developing district policy on encroachment permits?
2.
Is Director Lott prohibited from participating in
decisions concerning encroachment permits?

CONCLUSION: 1.
He is not prohibited from participating in decisions
establishing district policy if that policy affects his interests
in the same manner as it affects the public generally.
1g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—93—064
Page Two
2.
Director Lott is not prohibited from making or
participating in the making of decisions concerning the approval
or disapproval of encroachment permits on property other than his
own, unless those decisions will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on his own economic interests.
